findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is multiple prominent cystic lesions within the basal ganglia and periventricular regions, which exhibit low T1 and high T2 signal intensities [1]. These correspond to dilated perivascular spaces filled with gelatinous material [1].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the potential presence of leptomeningeal enhancement and a classic absence of significant surrounding vasogenic oedema or mass effect [1, 2]. The cystic spaces may appear slightly hyperintense on FLAIR imaging and can sometimes show restricted diffusion if the capsular contents are highly viscous [1].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a thick irregular rind of peripheral enhancement or massive vasogenic oedema to suggest a high-grade primary neoplasm [3]. Furthermore, there is no classic continuous smooth ring enhancement to suggest a mature pyogenic bacterial abscess [4].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of numerous non-enhancing pseudocystic perivascular lesions in the basal ganglia of an immunocompromised patient [1].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Infection (cryptococcosis in a patient with AIDS), given the prominent perivascular cystic lesions in the lenticulostriate region representing gelatinous pseudocysts [1]. In the setting of HIV/AIDS, cryptococcal fungi spread along the perivascular spaces, typically sparing the cortex and presenting without marked contrast parenchymal enhancement [1, 2].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Virchow–Robin (VR) spaces, however these normal anatomical variants should strictly follow cerebrospinal fluid signal on all sequences and remain fully suppressed on FLAIR [5]. In contrast, cryptococcal gelatinous pseudocysts can demonstrate hyperintensity on FLAIR sequences and may be accompanied by signs of active meningitis [1].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lacunar infarct, given they also present as small cystic spaces in the basal ganglia and deep white matter, typically in elderly patients with microvascular ischaemic disease [6, 7]. Missing an active cryptococcal infection by misattributing the findings to chronic microvascular ischaemia could delay life-saving antifungal therapy in a vulnerable patient.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to communicate the findings to the clinical team to facilitate urgent lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid cryptococcal antigen testing [1].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to confirm whether these cystic spaces are newly developed, and I would review the clinical record for a known history of HIV or other causes of severe immunosuppression [7, 8].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain with intravenous contrast if not already performed, as this modality is superior here for detecting subtle leptomeningeal enhancement and evaluating the true extent of the gelatinous pseudocysts [1].
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to carefully assess for leptomeningeal enhancement, alongside FLAIR and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) because the highly viscous mucopolysaccharide material of cryptococcal pseudocysts may uniquely demonstrate restricted diffusion and FLAIR hyperintensity [1].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Infectious Diseases and Neurology MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of the basal ganglia pseudocysts, the presence or absence of communicating hydrocephalus secondary to meningeal exudate, and any signs of active meningitis [1].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is multiple prominent cystic lesions within the basal ganglia and periventricular regions, which exhibit low T1 and high T2 signal intensities [1]. These correspond to dilated perivascular spaces filled with gelatinous material [1]. Supporting features include the potential presence of leptomeningeal enhancement and a classic absence of significant surrounding vasogenic oedema or mass effect [1, 2]. The cystic spaces may appear slightly hyperintense on FLAIR imaging and can sometimes show restricted diffusion if the capsular contents are highly viscous [1]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a thick irregular rind of peripheral enhancement or massive vasogenic oedema to suggest a high-grade primary neoplasm [3]. Furthermore, there is no classic continuous smooth ring enhancement to suggest a mature pyogenic bacterial abscess [4]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of numerous non-enhancing pseudocystic perivascular lesions in the basal ganglia of an immunocompromised patient [1].

The appearances are most in keeping with Infection (cryptococcosis in a patient with AIDS), given the prominent perivascular cystic lesions in the lenticulostriate region representing gelatinous pseudocysts [1]. In the setting of HIV/AIDS, cryptococcal fungi spread along the perivascular spaces, typically sparing the cortex and presenting without marked contrast parenchymal enhancement [1, 2]. The principle differential would be Virchow–Robin (VR) spaces, however these normal anatomical variants should strictly follow cerebrospinal fluid signal on all sequences and remain fully suppressed on FLAIR [5]. In contrast, cryptococcal gelatinous pseudocysts can demonstrate hyperintensity on FLAIR sequences and may be accompanied by signs of active meningitis [1]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lacunar infarct, given they also present as small cystic spaces in the basal ganglia and deep white matter, typically in elderly patients with microvascular ischaemic disease [6, 7]. Missing an active cryptococcal infection by misattributing the findings to chronic microvascular ischaemia could delay life-saving antifungal therapy in a vulnerable patient.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to communicate the findings to the clinical team to facilitate urgent lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid cryptococcal antigen testing [1]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to confirm whether these cystic spaces are newly developed, and I would review the clinical record for a known history of HIV or other causes of severe immunosuppression [7, 8]. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain with intravenous contrast if not already performed, as this modality is superior here for detecting subtle leptomeningeal enhancement and evaluating the true extent of the gelatinous pseudocysts [1]. I would specify T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to carefully assess for leptomeningeal enhancement, alongside FLAIR and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) because the highly viscous mucopolysaccharide material of cryptococcal pseudocysts may uniquely demonstrate restricted diffusion and FLAIR hyperintensity [1]. This should go to the Infectious Diseases and Neurology MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of the basal ganglia pseudocysts, the presence or absence of communicating hydrocephalus secondary to meningeal exudate, and any signs of active meningitis [1].
